The Python code correctly translates the Coq definitions. a_value computes i*i - i + 1. is_valid_triple checks the same conditions (bounds and divisibility by 3). count_valid_triples uses the same formula with integer division (// in Python matches Coq's / for integers). get_max_triples_spec returns the same boolean judgment based on whether n <= 2 or n > 2. Python's integer division and modulo for positive integers behave the same as Coq's Z operations.

Python mirrors Coq’s Z arithmetic: // and % with positive divisors match Z.div and Z.mod; chained inequalities match conjunctions; the count formula and conditional spec are implemented identically for all integer n, with the implication structure captured by the if/else.

Arithmetic, comparisons, and modulo/division match Coq Z semantics (with positive divisor). is_valid_triple uses equivalent chained inequalities and mod-3 check. count_valid_triples mirrors the same formula with // and %, and get_max_triples_spec branches identically on n<=2 vs n>2. Thus results coincide for all inputs.
